title: Tear Trough Complications: Why Are They So COMMON？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Tear Trough Complications： Why Are They So COMMON？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: Right now it's about doing what we need to do to get through today and tomorrow and the next week welcome to the aesthetics mastery show the show designed to help you raise the bar and thrive in your esthetics career of course more recently we've been a little bit more focused on…

segments.text: right now it's about doing what we need to do to get through today and tomorrow and the next week welcome to the aesthetics mastery show the show designed to help you raise the bar and thrive in your esthetics career of course more recently we've been a little bit more focused on this covert 19 crisis but today we're heading back into aesthetics and what are we talking about today we're gonna tackle periorbital edema that tricky complication that happens after tear trough treatment and could be quite upsetting for the client and can feel quite confusing and unnerving for the practitioner gonna go through what to do how to speak to a client and why I happens in the first place and then lastly we're gonna discuss Boris Johnson the UK prime minister who it was announced last night because we're filming on Tuesday went into ITU he had corona confirmed for about 10 days and the progression is quite unnerving for a lot of us we're asking questions like

have we been lied to should he have been lazing with people you know week and a half ago in Parliament lots of questions coming out and actually of course we wish him well but we wanted to kind of debate those out and understand the implications for us okay so I was first Terrace let's go in so I have had my teardrops done I think only twice actually remarkably because they last a long time for me but I actually had tear trough edema this came on later for me it was kind of like a few months later and it was quite upsetting because you do think yourself oh god you know I want to look better and now I look worse so why does it happen so there are many different reasons it can happen the first thing to know about this particular area is that you're dealing with the thinnest skin in your body particular in your face it's point to the millimeter thick in that area and then it's so any imperfection is really easy to see combined with that it's also an area where there is often a little bit of increased so it's probably for the same reasons to be honest the fact that the skin is so thin that any

build-up and fluid it becomes very obvious so you want to look out for people who have a tendency towards getting this problem so anyone who reports swelling in the morning when they first wake and actually all of us have it to a degree if you look at your face very carefully after lying flat for a few hours most people will have or increase and fluid in their face but if that shows physically clearing clearer in their tear trough then you want to be particularly careful about treating these people and maybe not treat them at all so patient selection is one of the reasons that people get it the fact that the area is inherently sensitive to build up a fluid is one and there's another reason that people get it the second one could be the type of products that people use and the placement or the injection technique that is used all of these things there isn't like and many things in aesthetic there's no body of evidence to give you a concrete perfect way of doing it they'll never cause a problem there are just tendencies one way or the other where you might have a slightly higher risk doing it one way versus using a different technique so a good clinician will modify these

techniques over time so that you build up a good spread of other ways of modifying that the treatment to the specific patient in front of you and gradually reduce your risk of getting this problem over time but it happens to all of us you will get the side effect at some point does edema tend to show up more for cannula or needle use in theory if you're using a cannula you're gonna be slightly more superficial even if you try and get deep and which is usually more painful and more uncomfortable it's more likely that you're gonna spend a lot of the time and a lot of the product will effectively go into the tzuf so the sub orbicularis oculi fat pad and we're mixing filler with fat and the other way of doing it with a needle is you're placing it deeper and you're putting the little bonuses of dermal filler on the periosteum sort of literally on the surface of the bone and then trying to prop up the fat from underneath and of course all of this is the mental model of what's going on and we don't hundred-percent know for sure the percentages are filler they get mixed in with a fat pad versus underneath the fat pad with different injectors using different techniques and trying with

different intent to place the filler in different places so a lot of this is built around mental models my experience is that you get less edema when using a needle and placing it deeper and but I'm sure there are others who feel differently and it will say there are other ways of mitigating the risk and of course you're always mixing the risk of that particular side effect with other might cause you to choose to have a cannula as well absolutely so everything has a trade-off it's a really good point and I often say that to be one I'm training is you should know you can't just say there's a perfect formula there's always a downside doesn't matter what your procedure is there's there are benefits and there are downsides of each thing that you do so if you're going to use a needle yes you'll get less bruising you'll get less edema but you're also going to have an increased risk of bruising and possibly an increased risk of vascular occlusion similarly if you use a cannula you might reduce bruising you may reduce the total number of vascular occlusions but you might increase the risk of a really big vascular occlusion because can enlist

you tend to put more in one place so that you've got it you know that you're getting somewhere in terms of your understanding when you can give pros and cons to each procedure rather than saying oh this is the right way or this is the right filler everything has a good side and a bad side but on the topic a fellow though we do only use one or two fellows which ones are they footed so tier so we're density 2 which has got FDA approval in the States you'll see it described as the only licensed filler in the UK that's a great little marketing spin from to yourself from T octane which makes everyone think oh they're doing something wrong but that doesn't really apply in the UK it's not the same license but it is a great advantage to know that they have validated their claims through a complex system such as FDA approval so it's definitely a really positive point for using SIOC same product and I have been using vah Bella for years and I know it really well and I still prefer I like the longevity you get with it I haven't experienced the downsides that people sometimes talk about in terms of reaction the factory audited all our patients and found exactly what the data

suggested we should get which is in Noreen five percent chance which is standard for dermal fillers for reactions so I'm confident in that product and I like the longevity so I use it the most unless and they recommend it the Juvederm recommended Allegan recommended for their and tit for periorbital treatment and that just if anyone's thinking about that study which showed that there were problems with the vie cross-range you don't have to go into it here but that was appalling science wasn't it well yeah I mean it's what it's worth having a look because I noticed that it's since we've mentioned might as well talk about it but the this all kicked off in probably about maybe three or four years ago now and it's a paper you still find online by art ciear tzi and you can read the paper and it's worth understand it's worth reading because the headline is very gripping it says icon of exactly but it's in the abstract it talks about a difference between I might get the figures wrong as haven't looked at it for a long time but a four percent reaction versus a nor point naught 5

percent reaction rate so they're saying we discovered that Juvederm the dividend by cross-range produces over a hundred fold increase in complications and that's particularly reactions but when you delve into a little bit deeper and you realize that they the number that they've generated it's always important to look at think of numbers as the output of a system or a process so the system or the process that they generated those numbers out are so different that you cannot possibly compare the outputted numbers and this is essentially the bottom of it which is they use reported data which is like the yellow card system that we use in the UK so as a GP if I prescribe you know and an antidepressant and someone has a reaction to it I'm supposed to fill out a yellow card report it and then that goes into a pool of data and then that becomes the post marketing findings so the post launch data that they start to modify risk with and so that's one way of producing a and a risk ratio but the other way of producing is that

you take a close cohort of your own patients and you follow them up really closely and you ask them for every side effects and complications they get now obviously those are ridiculously different methods of getting data on the topic and it's no surprise that you'd expect to get far fewer reports where you're relying on busy clinicians to fill out reports for every complication they get the truth is most of us probably let many of these complications slide because you're very busy doing other things so now we should report I think it's a good idea that we all try and report for this reason but you cannot possibly set up a scientific study and draw conclusions on two entirely different methodologies but this was shared as an abstract when viral amongst all the Facebook groups you know now in our circle everyone was sharing and it put the fear of God in people and in fact me too the first time I saw was like oh my god I've been using this stuff for years I read need to check it and we did we audited all out all our clients over about a year's a year-long period and we found thankfully the same rate of reaction as we'd expect from any dermal filler product which is reassuring especially as you've had the

treatment yourself and but that it's a there are other problems of the paper like they also weren't clear on their actual outcome measures so that they sometimes talk about infections and they treat it as infection but then they seem to include it in their data as a reaction sometimes it was they've talked about reactions and treating with antibiotics so it's really it was basically very poorly constructed study with a very scary conclusion which scared a lot of traditions and basically isn't worth the paper was written on in my humble opinion if a patient texts you or whatever or calls you and says right I've got let's say they were treated a week ago and they've got this edema now and it's kind of they say something like you know looks worse than before what would you do so I mean the first thing to realize is there are different reasons why you get edema so you they're not always caused by and the reaction for example which is our worst case scenario reacting as a product and if it's very soon after the procedure you might consider that it's post procedure every time you put the needle into the skin you will get a degree of reaction and that reaction is

caused by the procedures not caused by the product or matter what you inject said you may have injected say line and you still have some puffiness because you have a bruise remember bruising causes swelling - it's normal for your body to react to the presence of blood with inflammation and so bruising might confuse what you're seeing as well so the first thing is don't rush into it assuming a diagnosis is always the same diagnosis of edema sometimes it's normal information so in which case what you do you wait so it might be appropriate initially to wait particularly if there's been some bruising on the day the next the consideration would be is it infection if it's particularly focused on one side there was no bruising and then it seems to be swelling and getting more and more red you might be dealing with an infection rather than reaction as a general rule you'd expect an infection to be unilateral because it's pretty bad luck to get on both sides you could get on both sides but that's one of your differential as well and you then also got simply the one that's most common is that there's just basically a degree of increased and water retention on that side because

whatever reason their body doesn't let fluid drain easily from the cheek can be because you've got quite a strong orbicularis oculi retaining ligament and it could be also because the product is relatively superficial that you're seeing more of it and bad product is probably than one of the most common reasons because for some reason people still don't emphasize the product at now and you get taught the broadly speaking the technique you know one of these training courses with this 15 people watching and you're just generally trying to do everything in one day and it doesn't all go in and even a good clinician you know with good intent might use basically a bad product and there's loads of this going on and then 50 percent of their clients when they first start will have periorbital edema because the product is designed to hold moisture and add volume and it's not the right product to use in the tear trough so product might be another thing and finally reactions one to really know about because that one definitely needs treatment rapidly same as infection but you need to do something to decrease the amount of information so it might be appropriate to use steroids in some people but I would say most of these are

to do with a little bit too much fluid in which case there's a simple protocol you can go through which I'm sure we going to talk about so they've continued its a week at week afterwards it's suddenly start they thought it was you know they were fine with it for the first few days thought was just normal but now it seems to be persisting what would you say some next you recommended waiting but would you say wait for X number of days and then something else yeah I don't think I think it's reasonable not to get involved with anything invasive in the first few days because a lot of it could just be post procedural swelling so and especially if there's a bruise present you don't want to rush in with high waves because chances are it's going to get better on its own and bruising I would wait even up to a month I've had a number of people who had quite a upsetting amount of edema through one at one little area that bruised and then basically waited a month and then we're really happy with it so and but that's a long time to wait you got puffiness but the risk you're taking is that you dissolve it and get rid of it completely that causes more bruising that's not us

then you maybe try and retreat it and they just end up with the same thing happening again so discretion is the better part of valor as they're saying like doing nothing is often really appropriate so the first thing to try often is to select let it settle now I would gauge that depending on how my patient responds because if someone's freaking out over this and can't carry on with their life I'm not gonna for want of a better word quarantine them for a month and because of some swelling it might be better that they don't get the result they were after and you stop and if it's deeply upsetting but for most people waiting is a good idea that's the first thing I do now you can do something soon as still if you feel like this is just a little bit of product maybe you've been a little bit generous with a product and a little bit of puffiness which you can do a little massage so use that why you would massage when you've this basically too much filler in there yeah I mean it's it sounds because obviously on the day it often looks normal it's not like you've just inject a little Amber filler they've got a lump like you could do in other areas of the face this is this isn't quite as simple as injecting

too much it means for some reason the filler that is there is holding on to product are holding on to fluid a bit more so you can try and move the filler out of that area and take some of the fluid that's holding on with it so that's a low-risk thing to do and which is just a massage the area so yeah with so with gloved hands we use a little bit of arnica gel you just put it on your fingers and you massage down and out into the cheek and you my thinking is you're out you're underneath you're pushing it through little gaps in the ligament and out into the cheek and that will reduce the amount of filler a little bit and it will take some of the fluid with it now usually works immediately but if you haven't taken much product out and rebound and so you can ask your patient to do that at home as well so that would be the next step you let them go home and once a day massage the area out and that seems to help maybe 50% of people how many days after the treatment would you do the massage I'd be happy to do it if there's no sign of information it's and one of these simplest things is if you start the massage and it feels tender more tender than normal then

you've probably got a degree of information left so you might just want to leave that a bit longer and if you massage an area that's inflamed it tends to get worse so you it's better not to do anything if there's post procedure in Meishan so and a week has probably about as early as I'd consider doing it and two weeks might be better though quite often people are back a bit sooner than that because they're upset yeah okay and what happens if as it did with me that the swelling comes up months later what why is that okay so that is most commonly due to some sort of reactive process I think so it could it's most it's probably a delayed onset reaction so I know you've certainly had it once with a viral infection which happens with all dermal fillers and but it's basically there's inflammation going on in your whole system and some of those antipas some of those inflammatory and components settle in the dermal fellow and cause a bit of information and it was it's pretty mild and it seems to even sometimes lasts an

hour and then stop and but it's not something that if it's consistently swollen you'd need to do some treatment quite soon so there's an argument just to leave it and see what happens and most people settles on its own and I definitely keep stock of that data so that you're getting an idea what how often this happens and but the next step would be to try and reduce the body's response so now the big thing about this is you don't want to be reducing the body's response if there's an infection that needs fighting so I would be very cautious about recommending steroids to anyone who's having some information because you know you never know if they've got a brewing UTI or you know a viral infection and you're just about to make it worse so um I would want to make sure that the patient has zero signs of infection before I started decreasing the response of their immune system what happens if massaging doesn't work so it's probably appropriate then to if the particularly the patient is upset and doesn't want to wait because it you could slug you if it's mild sometimes a

bit more waiting might be okay if they are just hoping to get the result without having any more interventions and but I'd say usually after you've tried a couple of weeks of massaging at home you've massaged in the clinic it's there every day perhaps even slightly worse than you you're obviously in the realm of needing to reverse it so Hanna on Days is appropriate dissolve the product and get rid of it all and then you can discuss where that's worth try again maybe using a different technique if you've used a cannula might switch to a needle or putting less in you might change your product this is if the patients really desperate to get the result that they're after so let's move on to our corona based topic which today is Boris Johnson the UK prime minister who last night was admitted to ITU he's had corona for he's been diagnosed for ten days and he was admitted to hospital on Sunday night but then last night Monday he went that step further in Thai to you and people are doing various most people are being very supportive and saying come on Boris pull

through because it's quite anxiety making to think that the boss is what has also succumbed but some people are saying well why weren't we told this is blamele a cover-up or what was he doing in Parliament a week and a half ago and people are asking questions you know how bad is he so what do you think well I mean now I'm speculating a little bit about what he's like as a patient but I would imagine that I have a theory that the more in control you are of your life and generally general the less you want to relinquish control to anyone so I imagine he feels he's probably a bad patient who wants to soldier on and I don't think that it would be responsible of the government even to give the worst-case scenario as a potential outcome all the time because it's quite scary for all of us and so the fact that they said oh he's you know it's in the best place they're trying to give a reassuring message totally understandable I don't expect them to announce all the ups and downs and his blood results and you know that it could be heading in a bad direction but of course no one ends up in ITU and

that's just seriously sick so he is you know it's not a good situation do you think that from my lay perspective I'm thinking well maybe they're just giving him extra care because he's the boss maybe they're just kind of being overcautious though there will be people on this team I'm sure who were who were taking that line I imagine he will be pushing in the opposite direction and trying to stay in charge and keep doing what he wants to do so I think there'll be a balancing force I don't think that you that the Prime Minister will sitting ITU just as a precaution if he's generally well and there's definitely something going on and it's you know this virus the older you get has certain categories that are high risk and he's five isn't it yeah he went on for ten days this is quite well known that this risk seems to come in waves it's a really well described pattern of a fairly mild illness of temperature then you feel like you're getting over it and then suddenly it gets really bad around ten days in and so you know some I haven't got read up on that all the

detail but somebody with a cytokine storm this big response that the body kicks off then starts to injure the lungs and I really hope he's not in that bad situation but if he's in if he's on oxygen and he's in ITU then there's something going on with his lung function so he probably is but he's not an event Elise yeah is he as far as you know right now he's not on a ventilator okay most people in ITU with Kovac would probably be on CPAP which is continuous and positive airway pressure so they're adding a little bit of help to help you breathe taking off some of the energy required we know is on oxygen there's probably a CPAP machine I would have guessed but I don't know that for sure okay but he's now in a pretty bad category because we know that he's not the 85 percent who get it mildly he's in the 15 percent would get it more severely and of course of the 15 percent would get it more severely and currently the data is saying roughly 1% of them higher in the UK is actually four or five percent I think in the UK actually died from it so that's four or five percent of the

total so he's now in a category of thirty about a third of the people in his situation actually he might die so it's pretty high stakes I really hope that doesn't happen because actually I mean you know the show is not political but I feel like obviously you know many of us didn't vote for him but in recent weeks we've all just been like come on let's get together let's just band together and I think there was in some respects there was a good response to and economically to helping businesses with the furloughing which is to for those of you aren't in the UK colleagues who are in businesses who cannot function are being given the government 80% of their wage but then the in other respects we don't think the government did well you know in their in how in how quickly they responded to lockdown and all that as well so there's but I think generally speaking we're just like come on Boris

get out this because we need you yeah well that's exactly my point of view and you know I don't think that the thing is with the NHS is he's there's definitely perception and I'm not gonna get political here myself there's a perception that they are not the big the biggest party for investing in the NHS historically let me put it that way there's quite mildly people shouting into this but imagine that and but we're now in a situation where we're not gonna have another election and we've got what we've got a flawed we're all flawed but this is the best chance we've got so my attitude is we need to Park political differences focus on literally the here and now right in front of us what needs to be done and even the missed that you know I do believe there was a delay in reacting if you see how well Australia are doing they lock down earlier than us and they're suppressing cases they're gonna have far fewer deaths now they that the UK didn't do that and those are definitely once this is done and dusted we can look back and

say who did something wrong why they did it wrong was it incompetence and should we react accordingly but right now is not the time for that right now it's about doing what we need to do to get through today and tomorrow and the next week and then three weeks on and then once were on the other side of this curve and then we can start to thrash about who should have done a better job but right now it's all hands on deck to get through the crisis I think and not point-scoring from things you wish had been done differently over the last ten years it's not the right time and do you think that Boris should be relinquishing a bit more control because I think he's still trying to basically run the country for his ITU bad I don't think you'll be running the country with a CPAP machine on and I think that was that slightly when he was in hospital he was flying and I imagine a lot of this is put forward I mean you just imagine what it would be like for me to try and run the company from hospital bed you to run the company like you might say that you want to be but you cannot possibly do the same job so he's

not doing the same job but he's they don't want to say that he's that our Prime Minister has lost in battle and there's no one in charge so they won't do that until the very last moment and then they'll switch to confidently to someone else running the government if that's required mm-hm so I imagine a lot of those are statements of reassurance rather than pretending he's got his big desk and everything happening is normal with a CPAP machine on that's not happening okay well get well soon Boris we need you if you want to get your own personal protocol for getting through edema from tear trough treatments you can download a PDF from my website have a look in the link in the details below and that'll be there for you if you've got any thoughts or opinions on what we've talked about whether it be the political side or the non political side whether it's about tear trough edema or anything else we'd always love to hear from you so comment on this and we will get back to you and include it in the next show if we can thanks guys [Music]
